name: E-Learning (Self-Paced)
tagline: Asynchronous digital courseware that learners complete independently, on their own schedule.
whenToUse: Content is consistent and unlikely to change frequently
You need to reach a large, geographically distributed audience
The skill involves procedural steps that can be practiced in a simulation
Compliance or certification tracking is required
Learners have different start schedules or time zones
whenNotToUse: The task requires real-time coaching, mentoring, or nuanced judgment
Content changes faster than you can update the course
Learners have no reliable access to devices or internet
The primary goal is relationship-building or team alignment
strengths: Highly scalable — the same course serves hundreds or thousands
Consistent delivery — every learner receives identical content
Trackable completion and scores via an LMS
On-demand — learners can revisit content at any time
limitations: High upfront design and development cost
Poor fit for complex judgment tasks that require human feedback
Maintenance cost grows with content change frequency
designerNote: Self-paced e-learning shines when consistency matters more than conversation. If the task can be reduced to a set of decisions a learner can practice in a branching scenario, e-learning is often your most cost-effective long-term solution. The mistake is using it for 'soft skills' that require human modeling and live feedback — those need a live format.

name: ILT (Instructor-Led Training)
tagline: Live, in-person training with a facilitator — the original classroom format.
whenToUse: The content is complex, nuanced, or requires real-time coaching
Relationship-building between participants is part of the outcome
Immediate feedback on judgment and performance is essential
The audience is co-located and available at the same time
High-stakes skills where human accountability matters
whenNotToUse: The audience is geographically distributed across locations
Budget does not support travel, facilities, or facilitator costs
Content is purely informational and does not benefit from discussion
Learners need to revisit content on their own schedule
strengths: Highest fidelity for judgment-based and interpersonal skills
Enables real-time discussion, Q&A, and in-the-moment adjustment
Builds cohort relationships and shared context among participants
Facilitator can adapt pacing and depth to the room's response
limitations: Not scalable — cost and logistics multiply with audience size
No guarantee of consistency between facilitators or sessions
Travel, venue, and scheduling add significant overhead
designerNote: ILT is often over-used for content that e-learning could handle just as effectively. Reserve it for what only live interaction can deliver: real-time practice with a human observer, coaching on nuanced judgment, and situations where the relationship between participants IS the learning. If you can achieve the same outcome asynchronously, you probably should.

name: VILT (Virtual Instructor-Led Training)
tagline: Live, synchronous training delivered online — ILT-level interaction for distributed teams.
whenToUse: Your audience is remote or distributed across locations
You need ILT-level interaction but cannot justify travel costs
Content benefits from real-time discussion, breakouts, and Q&A
Sessions are 60–120 minutes (shorter than full-day ILT)
Learners are comfortable with digital collaboration tools
whenNotToUse: Learners have unreliable internet or low bandwidth
The session is purely informational — a pre-recorded video works better
Groups are very large (50+) where interaction becomes impractical
Hands-on practice requires physical materials or environments
strengths: Reaches distributed audiences at a fraction of ILT travel cost
Supports real-time interaction, polling, breakout rooms, and Q&A
Sessions can be recorded and made available asynchronously
More time-flexible than scheduling physical classroom days
limitations: Screen fatigue and digital cognitive load add friction
Harder to read the room and lose the energy of in-person delivery
Technical issues (connectivity, audio) disrupt the learner experience
designerNote: VILT is not just ILT on Zoom — it requires a deliberate redesign for the medium. Break content into segments of no more than 20 minutes before adding interaction. Use breakout rooms for every practice activity. Build in more explicit check-ins than you would in a physical room, and always designate a producer to manage the platform while you focus on facilitation.

name: Microlearning
tagline: Short, focused assets (2–7 minutes) designed to address a single performance need.
whenToUse: Reinforcing or refreshing a skill already introduced elsewhere
Performance support at or near the moment of need
Spaced repetition as part of a longer learning campaign
Mobile-first or time-constrained learner populations
Just-in-time reference for specific steps or decisions
whenNotToUse: The learner needs to build a foundational skill from scratch
The topic requires extended practice, coaching, or context
You are trying to cover a complex, multi-step domain comprehensively
The content does not stand alone — full course context is required
strengths: Fast to produce and easy to update compared to full courses
High completion rates due to the short format
Effective for spaced repetition and recall reinforcement
Works well on mobile devices and low-attention environments
limitations: Cannot stand alone for complex skill development
Risk of fragmenting learning that requires continuity
Can create a 'check the box' perception if used as a substitute for real training
designerNote: Microlearning is most powerful as the 'after' — reinforce what was taught in a full course with short weekly touchpoints. The mistake is using it to replace foundational training. A 3-minute video cannot build a complex skill; it can only refresh or support one that already exists. Use it as part of a campaign, not as the primary solution.

name: Video (Non-Interactive)
tagline: Recorded visual content — demonstrations, expert interviews, animations — without branching or interaction.
whenToUse: Demonstrating a physical process, software workflow, or equipment operation
Sharing expertise from an SME or leader at scale
Storytelling or case study walkthrough to create emotional connection
Supplemental content in a blended program
Pre-work or post-work in a flipped classroom approach
whenNotToUse: The learner needs to practice, not just observe
You need to verify comprehension or track meaningful progress
Content changes frequently — video is expensive and slow to update
The audience needs to ask questions or clarify in real time
strengths: High engagement when well-produced
Excellent for modeling behavior and demonstrating complex processes visually
Easy to distribute and consume asynchronously
Effective as part of a larger blended curriculum
limitations: Passive consumption — no built-in mechanism for practice or feedback
Production quality expectations are high and growing
Expensive and time-consuming to update when content changes
designerNote: Video is a delivery medium, not an instructional strategy. A 20-minute talking-head video has the same cognitive impact as a 20-minute lecture — which is to say, limited. Use video for what it does uniquely well: showing a physical demonstration, creating emotional connection through story, or delivering a message from leadership. Always pair video with a practice activity or reflection prompt.

name: Job Aid / Reference Card
tagline: A quick-reference tool used at the moment of performance — not a training format, but a performance support tool.
whenToUse: The task is infrequent enough that learners cannot be expected to memorize it
The stakes of error are high and a reference check is accepted practice
The task has predictable steps that fit on a checklist or flowchart
The learner already has the underlying skill — they just need a prompt
Digital or physical access to the aid is available at the point of need
whenNotToUse: The task requires understanding and making nuanced judgment calls
There is no reliable access to the aid during the actual task
The root cause is a skill gap, not a memory or recall gap
strengths: Fastest to produce of any format — hours, not weeks
Zero cognitive overhead at the time of use when well-designed
Reduces errors on procedural tasks better than training alone
Easy to update as processes change without a full redesign
limitations: Only effective when the user can access it at the moment of need
Cannot substitute for training when the skill itself is missing
Poorly designed aids add confusion rather than reduce it
designerNote: Before building a course, ask whether a job aid would actually solve the performance problem. If the gap is 'I forget what to do' or 'I need the steps in front of me,' a well-designed checklist or reference card often outperforms a 30-minute e-learning module — at a fraction of the cost. Job aids are systematically underused by IDs who default to courses for every request.

name: Blended Learning
tagline: A deliberate combination of formats — typically async pre-work plus live practice — designed to build complex skills over time.
whenToUse: The skill requires both foundational knowledge AND practiced application
The audience is distributed but the content is too complex for pure e-learning
Pre-work would make live sessions significantly more efficient
A sustained learning campaign matters more than a one-time event
You have the budget and timeline to coordinate multiple modalities
whenNotToUse: The content is one-time informational — a single format would suffice
Learners cannot commit to multiple touchpoints over time
The organization lacks the infrastructure to coordinate async and live components
strengths: Best of both worlds: scalable async pre-work with live coaching and practice
Learners arrive at live sessions already prepared — maximizing live time
Spaced repetition is built into a multi-touchpoint design
Highest overall effectiveness for complex, sustained skill development
limitations: Most complex to design, coordinate, and manage over time
Requires organizational buy-in and learner commitment across multiple sessions
Alignment between async and live components must be deliberate — gaps are costly
designerNote: Blended learning is the right answer for most complex skill development — but it is often used as a default rather than a deliberate decision. When designing a blended program, work backwards from the live session: what do learners need to have done, read, or practiced before they arrive? That pre-work is your async component. Do not design the e-learning first and then ask what to add live.

name: Podcast / Audio
tagline: On-demand audio content — expert discussions, interviews, or narrated learning — consumed during commutes and downtime.
whenToUse: The content is supplemental — reinforcing or expanding on other formal training
Learners have regular commute or travel time to fill
Expert interviews or thought leadership conversations add credibility
You want to support an informal, self-directed learning culture
The audience is already a podcast-listening, self-motivated population
whenNotToUse: The content is procedural and requires visual or tactile reference
You need to verify comprehension or track meaningful completion
The primary skill requires practice and feedback — not passive listening
Learners are in noisy environments or lack reliable audio access
strengths: Extremely low production cost relative to video or e-learning
Consumed during time that would otherwise be unproductive
Expert voices add credibility and authenticity
Supports informal learning culture and self-directed development
limitations: No mechanism for practice, interaction, or comprehension verification
Hard for learners to reference specific content later
Passive format — retention without a follow-up activity is low
designerNote: Podcasts work best inside a broader learning ecosystem, not as standalone training. If your organization has a culture of self-directed learning, a podcast series can supplement formal programs and keep topics alive between courses. Pair each episode with a one-page summary or a single discussion question to increase the chance that listening translates to action.
